issue: Recent wrong answers were not guaranteed to be shown newest to oldest by event time.
root_cause: The data layer built the wrong-answer list from an ascending-sorted attempt array and used slice(-5), which depends on upstream ordering and does not preserve explicit newest-first semantics. The payload also lacked a stable display timestamp.
effective_fix: Normalize attempt timestamps from all supported fields, sort the full incorrect-event set descending by timestamp, then take the first five records. Preserve ts and answeredAt in the report payload and defensively sort again in the UI before rendering.
failed_approach: UI-only sorting without fixing the report-data builder leaves cloud payloads and any other consumers with inconsistent ordering semantics.
risk_note: Week-over-week comparison logic may still mix display name and telemetry identity. That is a separate issue and should not be silently bundled into recent-wrong fixes.
verification_rule: For any recent-event feature, verify source event timestamps are preserved, full event sets are sorted before truncation, and rendered order is checked against explicit timestamps.
next_time_instruction: If touching parent-report or answer-history views, document data source, event grain, sorting key, dedupe key, and display order before patching.

issue: Long-running quality work regresses when project rules and lessons only exist in chat context.
root_cause: Repository-level engineering memory was missing outside subsystem-specific folders, so new iterations had no guaranteed root-level read/write loop for lessons, change history, and latest status.
effective_fix: Add a root AGENTS.md plus root logs/change_history.jsonl, logs/lessons_learned.jsonl, and reports/latest_iteration_report.md so each iteration has a mandatory pre-read and writeback target.
failed_approach: Relying on model memory or one-off prompts creates repeated rediscovery and repeated bug patterns.
risk_note: These files only help if future iterations read them first and keep them current.
verification_rule: Before meaningful edits, confirm the agent read AGENTS.md, change_history, lessons_learned, and latest_iteration_report; after edits, confirm all three writeback files were updated.
next_time_instruction: When a fix uncovers a reusable rule, convert it into a JSONL lesson with a concrete verification rule and a direct next-time instruction.

issue: After a wrong answer, pressing Enter could acknowledge the wrong-answer gate instead of re-checking the student's corrected answer.
root_cause: The wrong-answer state reused Enter as an acknowledge shortcut whenever focus was no longer in the answer input. After a submit interaction, focus could remain on the submit button, so the next Enter took the acknowledge branch instead of calling check().
effective_fix: After every wrong attempt, return focus to the answer input and treat Enter on the submit button the same as Enter in the answer field, so corrected answers are re-evaluated before any acknowledge shortcut runs.
failed_approach: Assuming the answer input always keeps focus after submit is unsafe in browser UI flows because buttons can retain focus and change keyboard behavior.
risk_note: This fixes the main Enter-path regression but does not change the product decision that moving to the next question after a wrong attempt still requires acknowledge or skip.
verification_rule: For any retryable form flow, test Enter with focus on the input and on the submit button after an error state. Confirm both paths re-run validation instead of triggering unrelated shortcuts.
next_time_instruction: When a wrong-answer gate and a submit action share Enter, verify focus transitions explicitly and prefer submit semantics over navigation semantics.

issue: Parent-report page had ~500 lines of inline domain logic duplicated from concept engines, making testing and maintenance difficult.
root_cause: All domain logic (weakness ranking, recommendation generation, copy text building, practice generation, report enrichment) was inline inside the HTML page IIFE, unreachable by automated tests.
effective_fix: Extract each domain into a standalone IIFE module on window.*, add script tags to load them before the page IIFE, replace inline functions with thin delegates that call through to shared engines with fallbacks, and add enrichReportData at both showDashboard and refreshReport entry points to pre-compute derived fields.
failed_approach: Attempting to replace 300+ lines inline with replace_string_in_file in one shot causes partial matches and orphaned code. Better to use PowerShell array slicing for large block removals.
risk_note: When using enrichReportData, weeklyFocus and recommendations are placed on report.d (not report root) because the page uses r = d.d as the main report object. Tests must check result.d.weeklyFocus, not result.weeklyFocus.
verification_rule: After extracting inline logic to shared engines, always: (1) add fallback in delegate functions, (2) verify enrichReportData is called in ALL data entry paths (not just the initial load), (3) run both spec tests and integration tests.
next_time_instruction: For large inline code extraction: first add script tags, then replace individual functions one at a time with delegates, then use PowerShell for bulk removal of orphaned code blocks. Never attempt to replace 300+ lines in one replace_string_in_file call.

lesson: Weakness cards should have clickable practice links, not just text descriptions. Use getTopicLink() from AIMathRecommendationEngine to resolve topics to module URLs. When DRYing duplicated logic (like moduleMap in renderRemedial vs TOPIC_LINK_MAP in recommendation_engine), prefer delegating to the shared engine. Always add analytics tracking for new clickable actions.
anti_pattern: Rendering next-action as plain text instead of a clickable link makes it impossible for parents to act on recommendations directly. Duplicating moduleMap across inline code and shared engines leads to drift when new modules are added.
effective_pattern: Delegate topic-to-URL resolution to getTopicLink() with fallback to star-pack. Add data-* attributes for analytics tracking on every action link.

lesson: The WoW comparison logic (prev week vs current week) depends on AIMathAttemptTelemetry which uses d.name as userId for localStorage lookup. When extracting to shared module, keep getPrevWeekAttempts as a separate function that wraps the telemetry call, making identity mapping testable. Always provide fallback inline logic in case the shared module fails to load. The WoW module only needs 3 exports: computeWoW (pure data transform), formatDelta (HTML formatting), getPrevWeekAttempts (telemetry wrapper).
anti_pattern: Computing prev-week stats inline inside renderDashboard makes it impossible to unit-test the comparison logic without a DOM. Also, the original inline code computed prevAvgMs but never displayed it ŌĆö dead code eliminated during extraction.
effective_pattern: Split WoW into pure computation (computeWoW) and telemetry access (getPrevWeekAttempts). The page wires them together. Tests can test computeWoW without mocking localStorage.

lesson: After extracting 10 shared engines from parent-report (iterations 5-11), all remaining inline code is view-layer: DOM manipulation, event handlers, HTML template rendering. Domain logic extraction is complete. The delegate-with-fallback pattern works well: each extraction adds ~20 lines of fallback but makes the domain logic independently testable. The extraction sequence matters: extract pure data transforms first (weakness, recommendation, report_data_builder), then time-windowed aggregations (wow, progress_trend, practice_summary), then output formatters (parent_copy, parent_advice). Test count grew from 10 ŌåÆ 42 across 11 iterations with zero regressions.
anti_pattern: Extracting interactive UI logic (practice card quiz flow, SVG rendering) into shared modules would create modules that still need DOM mocking, defeating the purpose. Stop extracting when only view-layer remains.
effective_pattern: For each extraction: (1) read inline code, (2) create pure-function IIFE module, (3) add script tag, (4) refactor inline to delegate with fallback, (5) create tests, (6) sync mirror, (7) validate + commit. One module per iteration keeps changes atomic and reviewable.

lesson: Question pages store attempt telemetry under a device UUID (u_abc123...) via AIMathCoachLog.getOrCreateUserId(), but parent-report was passing d.name (display name like 'Õ░ÅµśÄ') to listAttempts/getPrevWeekAttempts. This caused WoW comparison to always show 'no prior data'. The fix is to resolve the device UUID via getOrCreateUserId() for any localStorage telemetry query, and use d.name only for display and cloud API calls (recordPracticeResult).
anti_pattern: Using a human-readable display name as a storage key when a different system uses a UUID as the key. When two identity systems exist (cloud display name vs device UUID), always verify which key each API expects.
effective_pattern: Add a getDeviceUid() helper that resolves the correct localStorage identity, and use it consistently for all local telemetry queries. Keep d.name for cloud/display contexts only. Add a regression test that proves UUID resolves to stored data while display name does not.

lesson: The esc() helper in parent-report only escaped &, <, > but not quotes. When esc() output is used inside HTML attributes (data-weak-topic, href, etc.), unescaped quotes can break attribute boundaries and enable XSS. Standard HTML escaping must include double-quote (") and single-quote (') to be safe in attribute contexts. Current Chinese topic names don't contain quotes, but this is a latent vulnerability that could be exploited if data ever contains user-controlled strings.
anti_pattern: Escaping only angle brackets and ampersands while placing escaped output inside HTML attributes. The 3-char escape (&, <, >) is only safe for text content nodes, NOT for attribute contexts.
effective_pattern: Always escape all 5 HTML special characters: & < > " '. Add a regression test that verifies the esc() function includes quote escaping by reading the source code. When auditing escaping, check every call site to determine if output goes into text content or attribute values.

lesson: The inline practice answer checker used strict string comparison (normAns), so equivalent fractions like 2/4 were rejected when the stored answer was 1/2. Students who understand the concept get penalized for not simplifying. Fix: add parseFrac() + fractionsEqual() using cross-multiplication. Accept equivalent fractions but show a reminder about simplest form.
anti_pattern: Using string equality for math answers. Any numeric or fractional answer should be compared by value, not by string representation.
effective_pattern: Parse fraction strings (n/d) into {n, d} objects. Use cross-multiplication (a.n * b.d === b.n * a.d) for equivalence. Accept correct-but-unsimplified answers with a gentle simplification reminder. Add test cases for both equivalent and non-equivalent fractions.

lesson: Audited 8 escape/sanitize functions across 8 HTML pages. Found 3 patterns: (1) Full escaping with regex /[&<>"']/g ŌĆö safe in all contexts. (2) Partial escaping (& < > only) ŌĆö safe for text nodes, vulnerable in attributes. (3) No escaping (String() only) ŌĆö safe only for textContent, never innerHTML. The critical finding was exam-sprint escapeHtml() at line 815 which was type (2) but used in attribute context at line 1593 (data-qid). Pages with safe implementations: fraction-word-g5, commercial-pack1-fraction-sprint, interactive-g5-midterm1, interactive-g5-national-bank, report/index.html. No-escape safeStr() functions in exam-sprint, g5-grand-slam, and linear are currently safe because they're used only for textContent.
anti_pattern: Assuming all escape functions across pages are equivalent. Each page may have its own implementation with different coverage. Always audit all call sites when fixing an escape function.
effective_pattern: When auditing escape functions: (1) grep for all escape/sanitize function definitions, (2) check which HTML special chars each escapes, (3) check whether output is used in text or attribute context, (4) fix only those used in attribute context without full escaping. Add source-level regression tests that verify the function implementation includes required escaping.

lesson: Practice in parent-report had two modes: single (ÕåŹńĘ┤õĖĆķĪī) and quiz-3 (õĖēķĪīÕ░ÅµĖ¼ķ®Ś). Only quiz-3 called persistPractice. Single-mode silently dropped results. This broke the commercial flow: student practices ŌåÆ results should appear in summary. Fix: in goNext() for non-quiz mode, reset quizRecorded and call persistPractice per answered question. Also, practice results should write to local AIMathAttemptTelemetry (appendAttempt) so aggregate analytics can see them ŌĆö not just cloud recordPracticeResult.
anti_pattern: Only persisting results at quiz-end when single-practice mode also generates useful data. Assuming cloud writeback alone is sufficient when local analytics pipelines depend on local telemetry.
effective_pattern: Every practice interaction that yields a score/total should persist via both local telemetry (for analytics) and cloud (for report). Use source='parent-report-practice' to distinguish practice attempts from real quiz attempts. Reset quizRecorded flag per question in single mode to allow per-question persistence.

lesson: When explainWrongDetail and buildPracticeFromWrong only cover a few flagship kinds, the parent-report remediation loop degrades into generic advice for the rest of the existing bank. That weakens the commercial promise even if the links and writeback are correct. Existing bank families should have explicit remediation branches before introducing new content.
anti_pattern: Improving parent-report UI and writeback while leaving most wrong-answer kinds on a generic fallback. Parents see targeted cards, but the downstream explanation and similar-practice step still feels generic.
effective_pattern: Audit real bank kind families, add deterministic explanation/practice branches for uncovered existing kinds, and add regression tests that assert each family no longer falls through to the generic cause text. Keep generated answers integer-safe when possible and avoid hint text that trivially reveals the answer.

lesson: Handwritten kind lists are not enough for remediation coverage once multiple bank formats coexist. In this repo, bank payloads are exposed as window.*_BANK arrays in docs/<module>/bank.js rather than a shared const QUESTIONS wrapper. The safest regression pattern is to execute the real bank.js file in a VM sandbox, read the exported array from window, dedupe by topic+kind, and assert each representative bank family resolves to a non-generic explanation and non-empty fallback practice.
anti_pattern: Parsing bank.js with a hardcoded const QUESTIONS regex or testing only synthetic kind strings misses repo-specific wrappers and lets real module kinds drift away from remediation coverage.
effective_pattern: Load real bank.js payloads through vm.runInContext(), discover the exported bank array from window, and use bank-backed tests for commercial/life modules. Follow that with a future source-level audit so new bank kinds fail automatically when remediation mappings are missing.

lesson: A partial bank-backed audit is still too easy to outgrow. The durable gate is to scan every current docs/*/bank.js module, dedupe by topic+kind, and assert both explainWrongDetail and buildPracticeFromWrong stay non-generic across the whole repo. Some legacy bank files may not execute cleanly in vm because they end with a literal \n token after the assignment, so the loader needs a fallback that extracts the assigned JSON array directly.
anti_pattern: Assuming every bank.js file can be executed as-is in a VM, or limiting the audit to a hand-picked module list. That misses format outliers like interactive-g5-midterm1 and interactive-g5-national-bank and weakens the value of the gate.
effective_pattern: Use a two-step loader: try vm execution first, then fall back to parsing window.* = [...] assignments with a regex that tolerates trailing literal \n. Run this audit as part of the parent-report test suite so new bank kinds break CI immediately instead of silently shipping generic remediation.

lesson: The first screen is more commercially useful when it surfaces the top weak concepts directly beside the KPIs and next actions, instead of forcing the parent to open a deeper section to understand why the system is recommending action. The safest implementation is not a new ranking path; it is a compact view over the existing ranked weakness list using the same reason/action delegates and stable topic links.
anti_pattern: Adding a new first-screen weakness panel with its own ranking or copy rules. That creates drift between the quick summary and the deeper weakness/remediation sections and weakens trust when the messages diverge.
effective_pattern: Render at most 3 weakness summary cards from the already-ranked weakness array, reuse describeWeakReason() and nextAction(), and include a direct practice CTA. Guard it with a source-level regression test that checks the cap, the explanation text, and the practice link CTA.

lesson: Parents trust a first-screen recommendation faster when each weak-area card shows a tiny piece of concrete evidence, not just the label and suggested action. A short sentence using existing weakness signals like wrong-count and high-hint-count is enough to make the recommendation feel grounded without adding another chart or section.
anti_pattern: Making the first screen more persuasive by adding separate scoring logic or heavy analytics markup. That increases drift and cognitive load instead of making the recommendation more understandable.
effective_pattern: Keep the first-screen weakness card compact: topic, why weak, evidence sentence, what to do now, direct practice link. Reuse the existing weakness object fields and enforce the evidence line in the summary regression test so future refactors do not silently remove it.

lesson: Once the first-screen weakness evidence sentence exists, leaving it assembled inline in the page recreates the same drift problem the shared engines were meant to solve. The evidence sentence should live in the weakness engine beside reason and next-action text, so any future copy or scoring-field change has one source of truth.
anti_pattern: Keeping evidence text as page-local string assembly while reason and next action already come from the shared weakness engine. That creates a second formatting rule path and makes the quick summary more likely to diverge from the rest of the report.
effective_pattern: Expose a small `buildWeaknessEvidenceText()` helper from `AIMathWeaknessEngine`, reuse it from the parent-report page, and add a regression test that checks both the helper output and the pageŌĆÖs delegation to it.

lesson: Once the shared weakness engine owns the evidence sentence, deeper weakness tables and remedial cards should render that same sentence rather than reassembling wrong-count and hint-count copy inline. Reusing the same formatter keeps first-screen and deeper trust signals aligned when copy or weakness fields change.
anti_pattern: Letting deeper parent-report sections keep their own evidence string templates after the summary card has already moved to a shared formatter. That recreates drift across the same report page and makes copy changes easy to miss.
effective_pattern: Store shared evidence text on recommendation objects, render it directly in detailed remedial cards, and add a source-level regression test that fails if the old inline evidence template reappears.

lesson: If the browser must still write parent-report data directly, the least-bad interim pattern is a session-scoped runtime token with explicit setter/clear helpers, not a bundled global config token or a long-lived localStorage PAT. That reduces accidental exposure and persistence while preserving the current write flow until a backend-owned path exists.
anti_pattern: Reading a write token from `window.AIMathCloudSyncConfig.gistToken` or storing it persistently in localStorage. Both patterns make a repo-clean build still commercially unsafe because the secret path is browser-visible and sticky across sessions.
effective_pattern: Read the write token from sessionStorage, migrate and delete the legacy localStorage key once, expose `setCloudWriteToken()` / `clearCloudWriteToken()` for explicit runtime use, and add a regression test that fails if bundle/global token injection or persistent lookup comes back.

lesson: The smallest reversible step away from browser-owned parent-report writes is a dedicated backend registry keyed by normalized name plus server-validated PIN, not a broad auth-system rewrite. Keep the current name+PIN UX, move PIN verification and storage to the backend, and let the static frontend only send report_data/practice_event payloads to POST endpoints.
anti_pattern: Patching the browser write path in place with more client-side token rules, or preserving browser-side `entry.pin` comparison after adding a backend fetch endpoint. That keeps authority in the wrong tier and duplicates verification logic.
effective_pattern: Add SQLite-backed fetch/upsert endpoints with hashed PIN storage, pass the PIN from unlock/refresh/practice flows into the backend, return report data without the PIN, and gate the static frontend with a configurable backend base (`AIMATH_PARENT_REPORT_API_BASE`, `AIMATH_API_BASE`, or `?api=`). Add one backend endpoint test and one source-level frontend regression test so the main write path cannot silently fall back to direct Gist PATCH again.

lesson: When adding subscription-gated endpoints alongside existing free endpoints, build the adapter with automatic fallback: paid path (X-API-Key + student_id) tries the subscription-gated endpoint first, and on 402/401/404 automatically falls through to the free path (name+PIN). This prevents paid-path failures from being user-visible while the credential provisioning UI is still being built. Store paid credentials in sessionStorage (not localStorage) because they are session-scoped secrets that should not persist across browser restarts.
anti_pattern: Requiring the entire frontend to switch to the paid path at once, or storing API keys in localStorage where they persist indefinitely. Also: adding new endpoint auth patterns without writing deny-by-default tests that verify the gate is actually checked.
effective_pattern: Add setCredentials/clearCredentials/hasCredentials to the adapter, gate the paid path on both hasCredentials AND isPaid(), fall through to free path on any paid-path error, and add a source-level regression test that fails if credentials use localStorage or if the paid path is accessible without subscription check.

lesson: Iterative security hardening: first pass (iter 39) establishes architecture (adapter, subscription endpoints, credential flow), but typically leaves legacy code paths that still leak secrets or sensitive data. A dedicated second pass (iter 40) is needed to audit every remaining usage of the old infrastructure. Pattern: 1) search for all remaining usages of old auth mechanisms via source-level grep, 2) for each usage determine if it's read-only or write, 3) remove auth headers from read-only paths (public Gist reads don't need tokens), 4) strip sensitive fields (PINs) from uncontrolled data sources, 5) write source-level regression tests that grep the actual code to prevent reintroduction. Source-level tests are more durable than mock-based tests for security invariants because they catch problems at commit time regardless of runtime conditions.

When extending a dual-path adapter to cover a new operation (practice events), follow the established pattern exactly: check _isPaidAndCredentialed(), route to subscription-gated endpoint with X-API-Key, fall through to free path on error. The corresponding backend endpoint must reuse the same 3-gate pattern (get_account_by_api_key + ensure_subscription_active + _verify_student_ownership) and the same sanitizer for the data type. Removing dead exports is safe once confirmed by source-level grep: search all HTML and JS files for the symbol name, verify 0 callers outside the definition file, then remove from export block while retaining internal functions for gradual cleanup. Add source-level regression test that the export block does NOT contain the removed symbols.

When wiring a paid bootstrap path that enables backend writeback, the subscription state confirmed by the backend should be SESSION-SCOPED (in-memory variable), not persisted to localStorage. This prevents a user who opens the page without credentials from retaining paid status from a previous session. The pattern: URL params ŌåÆ sessionStorage credentials ŌåÆ async whoami validation ŌåÆ in-memory _backendPaidStatus ŌåÆ getEffectiveSub() override. Three independent gates protect the paid path: (1) credentials in sessionStorage, (2) isPaid() returns true, (3) backend rejects invalid keys on every API call.
anti_pattern: Using activatePaidPlan() or confirmPayment() to sync backend-confirmed subscription state ŌĆö these persist to localStorage and survive across sessions, creating a privilege escalation vector where a user retains paid access after their subscription expires.
effective_pattern: Use an in-memory variable (_backendPaidStatus) set by syncFromBackend() and cleared on page unload. Hook into getEffectiveSub() between the hardcoded override (UNLIMITED_STUDENT_NAMES) and the localStorage fallback. Strip credentials from URL via history.replaceState immediately after reading them ŌĆö prevents sharing, bookmarking, or browser history exposure of API keys.

lesson: When wiring a paid bootstrap path that enables backend writeback, the subscription state confirmed by the backend should be SESSION-SCOPED (in-memory variable), not persisted to localStorage. This prevents a user who opens the page without credentials from retaining paid status from a previous session. The pattern: URL params to sessionStorage credentials to async whoami validation to in-memory _backendPaidStatus to getEffectiveSub() override. Three independent gates protect the paid path: (1) credentials in sessionStorage, (2) isPaid() returns true, (3) backend rejects invalid keys on every API call.
anti_pattern: Using activatePaidPlan() or confirmPayment() to sync backend-confirmed subscription state - these persist to localStorage and survive across sessions, creating a privilege escalation vector where a user retains paid access after their subscription expires.
effective_pattern: Use an in-memory variable (_backendPaidStatus) set by syncFromBackend() and cleared on page unload. Hook into getEffectiveSub() between the hardcoded override (UNLIMITED_STUDENT_NAMES) and the localStorage fallback. Strip credentials from URL via history.replaceState immediately after reading them - prevents sharing, bookmarking, or browser history exposure of API keys.

lesson: When passing credentials from a server-side APP to a browser-facing page, never put long-lived API keys in URL params. Instead use a short-lived, single-use bootstrap token: APP calls POST /bootstrap server-side with real API key to get an opaque token (secrets.token_urlsafe(32), 5-min TTL, stored in-memory dict). APP puts ?bt=<token> in URL. Frontend exchanges token via POST body to get real credentials. Token is popped (consumed) on first use. The exchange endpoint must re-validate subscription to prevent stale tokens from granting access after subscription expiry.
anti_pattern: Passing raw api_key in URL params (?api_key=xxx) exposes it in browser history, server access logs, referrer headers, analytics beacons, and shareable deep links. Even with history.replaceState() stripping, the key is visible during navigation and in the Address bar briefly.
effective_pattern: Bootstrap token exchange: (1) server-side POST with real X-API-Key -> opaque token, (2) URL carries only short-lived token, (3) frontend POST exchanges token for credentials via response body, (4) token is single-use (dict.pop), (5) frontend actively rejects raw api_key in URL with user warning. Source-level test window size must account for the full endpoint body (use 1000+ chars, not 800).

lesson: When adding rate limiting to bootstrap/exchange endpoints: (1) Use per-IP sliding window with separate limits per endpoint type (bootstrap stricter since it creates server state, exchange more generous since tokens are already single-use). (2) Add per-account outstanding token cap to prevent token flooding separate from IP rate limits. (3) Check rate limit BEFORE auth gates so even invalid requests count against the limit. (4) Use HTTP 429 with descriptive detail for limit hits. (5) Source-level tests need larger window sizes when endpoint body grows ŌĆö use 1500+ chars for endpoints with rate limiter + token cap logic. (6) Tests should temporarily lower limits and clear rate limit state to avoid flaky tests.
anti_pattern: Checking rate limit AFTER auth validation lets unauthenticated attackers probe without rate limit consequences. Adding rate limiting without token caps lets authenticated users flood the token store.
effective_pattern: Rate limit check runs first (before auth gates), per-account cap runs after auth (needs account_id), both return 429. Tests use fixture-scoped cleanup and temporarily lowered limits for deterministic behavior.

lesson: When moving from in-memory dict to DB-backed token store: (1) Store SHA-256 hash of token, never the raw token ŌĆö defense in depth against DB access. (2) Use _consume_bootstrap_token() with SELECT + UPDATE pattern (check unconsumed + unexpired, then set consumed_at). (3) Rate limiter moves to its own table (rate_limit_events) with key+ts columns and sliding window via DELETE + COUNT + INSERT. (4) Tests that previously manipulated in-memory dicts must switch to DB operations ŌĆö UPDATE for expiry manipulation, DELETE FROM for state clearing. (5) Source-level JS tests that check for '.pop(' must update to check for '_consume_bootstrap_token' since the consumption pattern changed. (6) Add a DB persistence test that verifies the token exists in DB and consumed_at is set after exchange. (7) Cleanup expired tokens with 2x TTL buffer to handle edge cases.
anti_pattern: Storing raw tokens in DB (allows token theft from DB access). Using in-memory dicts that are lost on server restart. Tests that reference module-level dict attributes (_bootstrap_tokens, _rate_limit_store) which break when the storage backend changes.
effective_pattern: Hash tokens before storing. Use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS + CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS in init_db(). Tests manipulate DB rows directly via conn = setup_server.db(). Source-level tests check for function names (_consume_bootstrap_token) not implementation details (.pop).

lesson: When building a login UI that feeds into an existing multi-step token exchange flow: (1) Raw api_key from login response must stay in closure scope only - declare as local var in the innermost practical scope, use for bootstrap X-API-Key header, then let it go out of scope. (2) readReportSnapshot() returns {data: report_payload, cloud_ts, source} - must pass entry.data (not entry) to showDashboard() since showDashboard checks d.d. (3) Clear password from DOM immediately after successful exchange. (4) Source-level security tests that count adapter.setCredentials calls must use regex with trailing ( to match actual calls, not property access checks like !adapter.setCredentials. (5) Collapsible <details> element with CSS arrow rotation on toggle is a low-risk, accessible UI pattern for optional login sections.
anti_pattern: Passing raw readReportSnapshot() entry to showDashboard() without extracting .data first. Counting method references (adapter.setCredentials) instead of method calls (adapter.setCredentials() with paren) in source-level tests. Storing loginApiKey in any persistent storage even temporarily.
effective_pattern: 3-step flow (login->bootstrap->exchange) keeps raw api_key ephemeral. Error responses (401/402/403) handled individually with user-facing Chinese messages. Button disabled during async flow, re-enabled on ALL error paths (counted 5+ re-enable points). Auto-load report on success via readReportSnapshot reduces friction.

lesson: When adding rate limiting to an auth endpoint: (1) Rate limit check MUST fire BEFORE credential validation to avoid information leakage (attacker cannot distinguish valid-username-wrong-password from rate-limited). (2) The 429 response body must contain only a generic message, never credentials or username. (3) Login deserves a stricter limit than bootstrap (5/min vs 10/min) because login is the primary brute-force target. (4) Reusing the existing _check_rate_limit(key, max) pattern with a new key prefix (login:{ip}) is the minimal safe change. (5) Test must verify that a wrong-password attempt AFTER rate limit is exceeded returns 429 (not 401, which would leak that the IP was rate-limited but credentials were checked).
anti_pattern: Checking credentials before rate limit (leaks valid usernames via timing or response code differences). Returning detailed error info in 429 response. Using the same rate limit threshold for login as for authenticated endpoints.
effective_pattern: Rate limit as first operation in endpoint handler. Generic 429 detail message. Stricter limit for unauthenticated credential-guessing endpoints. Test both the rate limit itself AND the ordering guarantee (rate limit before credential check).

lesson: When adding rate limiting to a login endpoint: (1) Rate limit check MUST fire before ANY credential validation (DB lookup, password check) to prevent timing attacks that reveal whether a username exists. (2) Login rate limit should be stricter than bootstrap/exchange (5/min vs 10/20) since login involves password guessing. (3) The 429 response message must be generic - never include username, password, or api_key in the response body. (4) Reusing existing _check_rate_limit(key, max) infrastructure is zero-risk - just add a new constant and a 3-line rate limit block at the top of the endpoint. (5) Test the ordering property explicitly: exhaust rate limit with bad credentials (getting 401s), then verify next attempt returns 429 (not 401), proving the rate limiter fires first. (6) JS source-level test can verify ordering by checking that _check_rate_limit appears before WHERE au.username in the source text.
anti_pattern: Checking rate limit AFTER credential validation (leaks timing info about valid usernames). Using the same rate limit as bootstrap (10/min is too generous for password guessing). Including error details in 429 response.
effective_pattern: Rate limit fires first (before any DB query). Stricter limit for login (5/min). Generic 429 message. Three-test coverage pattern: (a) happy path rate limit, (b) ordering proof, (c) no-leak proof.

lesson: Account-level lockout complements IP-based rate limiting by preventing credential stuffing from distributed IPs. Key design: (1) lockout check fires AFTER IP rate limit but BEFORE credential validation ĪX this prevents the lockout itself from leaking whether a username exists. (2) Record failures for both invalid usernames and wrong passwords to avoid username enumeration. (3) Clear ALL failure records on successful login to prevent stale lockouts. (4) Use a separate table (login_failures) from rate_limit_events to keep concerns isolated. (5) HTTP 423 (Locked) is semantically distinct from 429 (Too Many Requests), allowing clients to differentiate IP-based vs account-based throttling.
